title: Strong Spectral Gap for Geometrically Finite Hyperbolic Manifolds
speaker: Dubi Kelmer
abstract: In this talk I will describe new results establishing the existence of a strong spectral gap for  geometrically finite, n dimensional, hyperbolic manifold with critical exponent greater than (n−1)/2. This settles a conjecture of Mohammadi and Oh from 2015, and is based on joint work with Osama Kahlil and Pratyush Sarkar.
tags: Joint IAS/PU Groups and Dynamics Seminar

proof_stages.label: Geometrically finite quotients and scalar spectral gap
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes a geometrically finite discrete subgroup Gamma of the isometry group of hyperbolic space, recalls the Laplacian spectrum on the infinite-volume quotient, introduces the critical exponent delta_Gamma, and identifies the ordinary spectral gap as the separation of the bottom eigenvalue from the rest of the spectrum.

proof_stages.label: Homogeneous A-flow and scalar matrix coefficients
proof_stages.summary: The quotient, unit tangent bundle, and frame bundle are written as homogeneous spaces involving G, K, A, and M, so geodesic and frame flow become A-actions, and for K-invariant functions the scalar spectral decomposition gives explicit correlation decay through matrix coefficients.

proof_stages.label: Complementary series as non-tempered spectrum
proof_stages.summary: The representation L^2(Gamma\G) is split into tempered and non-tempered parts, with the latter described by complementary series pi_{sigma,s} parametrized by irreducible M-types sigma, where the spherical case recovers Laplace eigenfunctions and nontrivial sigma create the extra frame-flow obstructions.

proof_stages.label: Strong spectral gap theorem
proof_stages.summary: Strong spectral gap is formulated as a uniform exclusion of complementary series near s=delta_Gamma, plus exclusion of nontrivial M-types at s=delta_Gamma, and the main theorem states that every geometrically finite Gamma with delta_Gamma>n/2 satisfies this condition, with surrounding discussion of conjectural uniform refinements and motivating known cases.

proof_stages.label: Dynamical mixing input and geodesic-flow obstruction
proof_stages.summary: Known exponential mixing for the Bowen-Margulis-Sullivan geodesic flow, Mohammadi-Oh's equivalence, and Edwards-Oh asymptotics show that complementary-series main terms vanish on M-invariant vectors, while recent exponential mixing for frame flow supplies the stronger dynamical input needed to detect all M-types.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to spectral measures by M-type
proof_stages.summary: For a fixed M-type sigma the non-tempered spectrum is expressed as a direct integral over the complementary-series parameter s with a control measure, and strong spectral gap is reduced to proving that these measures vanish on one interval below delta_Gamma independent of sigma.

proof_stages.label: Laplace-transform continuation of matrix coefficients
proof_stages.summary: Laplace transforms of matrix coefficients of fixed K-type test functions are evaluated using the complementary-series asymptotic to obtain an explicit meromorphic integral whose possible poles encode spectral mass, while frame-flow exponential mixing analytically continues the same transform into a left half-strip.

proof_stages.label: Stieltjes inversion and K-type nonvanishing
proof_stages.summary: A Stieltjes inversion argument turns absence of poles into vanishing of the spectral measure, and an explicit choice of a K-type tau for each sigma makes the leading coefficient nonzero, completing the proof strategy and leaving effectiveness and uniformity as further questions.

examples.name: Free hyperbolic space with trivial Gamma
examples.note: In response to a question about scattering poles, the trivial-group/free-space case is used to illustrate that bounds coming from the tempered spectrum need not be sharp for analytic test functions.
examples.engagement_reason: the trivial-Gamma free hyperbolic space is invoked in a question as a comparison for scattering poles, with only remembered facts stated

passes.reason: Trivial Gamma gives bare hyperbolic space, the maximally symmetric minimum-growth quotient, invoked to show the tempered-spectrum decay bound need not be sharp.

examples.name: Finite-volume lattice case and n=3 unitary-dual picture
examples.note: The lattice case, especially the sketched n=3 complementary-series picture, is presented as a reason one might expect a strong spectral gap beyond finite volume, because the endpoint delta=n visibly leaves a gap in the non-tempered spectrum.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies the unitary-dual picture in the lattice/n=3 case, identifies delta and lambda0, lays out complementary-series parameter intervals by sigma, and uses this to locate the spectral gap

passes.reason: A finite-volume lattice has delta equal to n and lambda_0 equal to 0, so it is the maximal-critical-exponent boundary case used as a benchmark.
The finite-volume lattice case has δ=n and λ0=0, the endpoint case in the unitary-dual picture where the spectral gap is clear.
